title: Agentic Verification for Ambiguous Query Disambiguation
authors: Youngwon Lee Seung-won Hwang11footnotemark:1Ruofan Wu†Feng Yan†Danmei Xu Moutasem Akkad Zhewei Yao Yuxiong HeSnowflake AI Research11footnotemark:1Seoul National University†University of HoustonWork done while visiting Snowflake. Correspondence to:seungwonh@snu.ac.kr.
abstract: In this work, we tackle the challenge of disambiguating queries in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to diverse yet answerable interpretations. State-of-the-arts follow a Diversify-then-Verify (DtV) pipeline, where diverse interpretations are generated by an LLM, later used as search queries to retrieve supporting passages. Such a process may introduce noise in either interpretations or retrieval, particularly in enterprise settings, where LLMs—trained on static data—may struggle with domain-specific disambiguations. Thus, a post-hoc verification phase is introduced to prune noises. Our distinction is to unify diversification with verification by incorporating feedback from retriever and generator early on. This joint approach improves both efficiency and robustness by reducing reliance on multiple retrieval and inference steps, which are susceptible to cascading errors. We validate the efficiency and effectiveness of our method, Verified-Diversification with Consolidation (VerDICt), on the widely adopted ASQA benchmark to achieve diverse yet verifiable interpretations. Empirical results show that VerDICt improves grounding-aware F1\textrm{F}_{1} score by an average of 23% over the strongest baseline across different backbone LLMs.

paragraphs: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG; Lewis et al., 2020) is expected to complement large language models (LLMs), trained on static data, when they struggle to provide reliable responses without external retrieval. This challenge is particularly evident in enterprise settings, where queries are asked on a domain-specific and potentially evolving corpora, inaccessible during LLM training. In such cases, RAG must (1) disambiguate short, vague user queries and (2) retrieve supporting evidence, to ensure the verifiability of the generated response.

A line of work on handling ambiguous question answering (Min et al., 2020; Cole et al., 2023; In et al., 2024) adopted a Diversify-then-Verify (DtV) pipeline, which first generates diverse interpretations of the query using an LLM and then retrieves supporting documents using interpretations as search queries. However, this process often leads to ungrounded or noisy interpretations, particularly when LLMs struggle with domain-specific or evolving meanings. For example, as shown in Figure 1(a), the query “What is HP” may refer to Hewlett-Packard, horsepower, or Harry Potter. LLMs trained on general knowledge would suggest all three, while an enterprise corpus may contain no references to Harry Potter. In other words, this interpretation is ungrounded. However, DtV still retrieves documents for all three interpretations, introducing noise from unnecessary retrieval. Figure 1(a) illustrates DtV, where the verification step comes after retrieval to prune out noisy passages, though “pseudo-interpretations” have already negatively influenced the process.

To address these limitations, we propose VerDICt (Verified-Diversification with Consolidation), a unified framework that integrates diversification and verification: VerDICt is agentic, in the sense that we ask feedback from retriever and generator to ensure all disambiguations are grounded upfront, as in Figure 1(b). This joint process, by avoiding noisy and unnecessary retrieval of ungrounded interpretation, mitigates cascading errors while improving efficiency. Specifically, agentic feedback from two sources play a critical role in the process:

• Relevance feedback from the retriever: We use a retriever to obtain a set of passages UqU_{q}, that are relevant to qq and encompass all interpretations found in the corpus. This retrieval-augmented diversification, as also employed in RAC (Kim et al., 2023), naturally filters out unsupported interpretations, such as “Harry Potter,” as it is irrelevant, or may become obsolete as corpus evolves.

• Execution feedback from the generator: Figure 1(b) illustrates why relevance alone does not guarantee that a question can be answered, necessitating execution feedback. Passage p2p_{2}, though relevant to Hewlett-Packard as it describes its products, cannot provide an answer to the given question. This motivates us to leverage the generator feedback to decide whether the question can be answered from the passage, based on which we prune out p2p_{2}.

Lastly, since feedback signals themselves can also be noisy, we introduce a consolidation phase to leverage consistency. By aggregating multiple feedback signals through clustering, VerDICt further mitigates potential noises. This phase does not incur an additional feedback cost while enabling a more robust decision-making.

As a bonus, this inherently grounded design of VerDICt, by tightly connecting each interpretation with its supporting passage, can be evaluated for verifiability metrics popular in RAG (Li et al., 2023a; Liu et al., 2023), such as citation quality. We use both existing and grounded metrics, to show ours disambiguation is grounded and accurately answered.
Our key contributions are as follows:
• We introduce VerDICt, a novel framework that unifies diversification and verification, enabled by an agentic approach of leveraging feedback from the retriever and the generator.
• We demonstrate that VerDICt improves efficiency by reducing unnecessary retrieval and verification steps, and also effectiveness, by mitigating cascading errors common in DtV.

• We empirically validate VerDICt on widely adopted ambiguous QA benchmark, ASQA (Stelmakh et al., 2022), with average gain of 23% in F1\textrm{F}_{1} score across different backbone LLMs.111The gain is computed against the best baseline for each backbone LLM. We also release our code and verifiability evaluation framework to facilitate future research.
This section overviews existing work on DtV workflow and the line of research on using retriever as environment.

subsections.title: 3.1 Problem Formulation
paragraphs: Our formulation adheres to the conventional ambiguous question answering setting, where the primary objective is to handle ambiguity in user queries effectively. Specifically, given an ambiguous question qq, the task of diversification involves identifying a comprehensive set of possible interpretations 𝒬={q1,q2,⋯,qN}\mathcal{Q}=\{q_{1},q_{2},\cdots,q_{N}\}, each representing a distinct and valid meaning of the original question qq.

The goal then extends to determining corresponding answers 𝒴={y1,y2,⋯,yN}\mathcal{Y}=\{y_{1},y_{2},\cdots,y_{N}\} for each interpretation qiq_{i}, ensuring the response is grounded on retrieved supporting evidence. The established evaluation protocol for such task involves comparing the model-generated predictions 𝒬^\hat{\mathcal{Q}} and 𝒴^\hat{\mathcal{Y}} against the ground-truth set of interpretations and answers, 𝒬\mathcal{Q} and 𝒴\mathcal{Y}.

subsections.title: 3.2 Baselines: DtV
paragraphs: In this section, we formally describe the DtV workflow, using the most recent work In et al. (2024) with state-of-the-art performance as reference. DtV first diversifies the query, or, identifies pseudo-interpretations 𝒬^={q^1,q^2,…,q^M}\hat{\mathcal{Q}}=\{\hat{q}_{1},\hat{q}_{2},\dots,\hat{q}_{M}\} that disambiguate the meaning of the original ambiguous query, by prompting LLM with instructions IPI_{\textrm{P}} without accessing any retrieved knowledge:

Each generated pseudo-interpretation q^i\hat{q}_{i} in 𝒬^\hat{\mathcal{Q}} are then used as a search query to retrieve top-kk supporting passages, the union of which forms a universe UU of relevant documents

where the subscript 𝒬^\hat{\mathcal{Q}} indicates that the universe U𝒬^U_{\hat{\mathcal{Q}}} is derived from the set of pseudo-interpretations 𝒬^\hat{\mathcal{Q}}. As pseudo-interpretations can be ungrounded and the resulting U𝒬^U_{\hat{\mathcal{Q}}} can be noisy, verification phase follows, to examine each pseudo-interpretation with universe U𝒬^U_{\hat{\mathcal{Q}}}. After this phase, U𝒬^U_{\hat{\mathcal{Q}}} is reduced to a verified subset UVU_{V}, from which disambiguated queries are answered.
We identify the challenges and inefficiencies in DtV line of work as follows:
1. U𝒬^U_{\hat{\mathcal{Q}}}: Each interpretation 𝒬^\hat{\mathcal{Q}} incurs at least one retriever call, some of which are ungrounded and could introduce noises.

2. UVU_{V}: Verified UVU_{V} requires to process all (q^,p)(\hat{q},p) pairs, for example, inducing |𝒬^||\hat{\mathcal{Q}}| Verify calls with input size of 𝒪​(|U𝒬^|)\mathcal{O}(|U_{\hat{\mathcal{Q}}}|) each.222Pruning U𝒬^U_{\hat{\mathcal{Q}}} in advance was used in In et al. (2024), though asymptotic cost remains unchanged. We discuss this in Section 4.2. Plus, such long-context verification requires a powerful LLM model, which increases cost and hinders applicability.
U𝒬^U_{\hat{\mathcal{Q}}}: Each interpretation 𝒬^\hat{\mathcal{Q}} incurs at least one retriever call, some of which are ungrounded and could introduce noises.

UVU_{V}: Verified UVU_{V} requires to process all (q^,p)(\hat{q},p) pairs, for example, inducing |𝒬^||\hat{\mathcal{Q}}| Verify calls with input size of 𝒪​(|U𝒬^|)\mathcal{O}(|U_{\hat{\mathcal{Q}}}|) each.222Pruning U𝒬^U_{\hat{\mathcal{Q}}} in advance was used in In et al. (2024), though asymptotic cost remains unchanged. We discuss this in Section 4.2. Plus, such long-context verification requires a powerful LLM model, which increases cost and hinders applicability.
We illustrate VerDICt with two distinctions: Verified Diversification (Section 4.1) and Consolidating Feedback (Section 4.2). In Section 4.3, we provide grounded evaluation protocols.

subsections.title: 4.1 Verified Diversification
paragraphs: Unlike DtV, we ensure that 𝒬^\hat{\mathcal{Q}} includes only grounded interpretations by leveraging agentic feedback from both the retriever and the generator. Unlike Eq. 1, where pseudo-interpretations are conditioned solely on the ambiguous query qq, our approach conditions on both the query qq and the corpus CC. This ensures that each interpretation q^∈𝒬^\hat{q}\in\hat{\mathcal{Q}} is explicitly grounded to at least one passage from the corpus, ensuring both relevance to qq, using the relevance feedback of retrieving UqU_{q}, and answerability of the interpretation.

Note that Eq. 5 jointly infers whether the retriever can retrieve a relevant passage (relevance) and an answer to qq can be generated from the retrieved passage. As a result, interpretations 𝒬^\hat{\mathcal{Q}} and answers 𝒴^\hat{\mathcal{Y}} are obtained simultaneously, along with the list of passages 𝒫^\hat{\mathcal{P}} that support each identified question-answer pair.

“Relevance feedback from the retriever” in Figure 2 shows how we first retrieve UqU_{q} with a single round of retrieval with the original query qq before generating interpretations. To ensure high recall within the universe, we use q′q^{\prime} relaxed as below:

With relaxed q′q^{\prime} and choice of a larger kk for retrieval,333kk was empirically tuned to 20, which nearly matched G-F1\textrm{F}_{1} (described in § 4.3) from top-100 with GPT-4o backbone. we further increase the diversity and coverage of the interpretations while keeping the process computationally efficient, marked as “relevance feedback from the retriever” in Figure 2.

Execution feedback from the LLM generator in Figure 2 prunes interpretatons that cannot be answered. Conditioned on our high-coverage universe UqU_{q}, the LLM generator is prompted to identify interpretations 𝒬^\hat{\mathcal{Q}} and corresponding answers 𝒴^\hat{\mathcal{Y}} from each passage.

This ensures the answerability of each disambiguated query q^i\hat{q}_{i}, given the passage p^i\hat{p}_{i} from which it was derived: For example, in Figure 2, passages like p2p_{2}, which mentions HP products but cannot answer the query asking what HP is–are effectively pruned through failed execution of generating question-answer pair. In contrast, with passages like p1p_{1}, a valid question-answer pair is generated. Such execution feedback is formalized as follows,
of which the LLM prompt IEI_{\textrm{E}} can be found in Figure 6.

From a practical standpoint, Eq. 8 can be executed in parallel, with each LLM call processing a single passage at a time. This minimizes the input sequence length for each call, optimizing both latency and computational overhead while reducing hallucination in less capable models.

Table 1 provides comparison of asymptotic cost of retrieval and LLM calls in VerDICt and DtV. First, while VerDICt makes a single call to the retriever as both builds on UqU_{q}, DtV issues several search queries, proportional to the number of pseudo-interpretations |𝒬^||\hat{\mathcal{Q}}|. Next, the number of LLM calls and input size of each call, measured in terms of the number of passages, also shows the efficiency of VerDICt. DtV provides several passages as a list input, increasing the context size and the chance of introducing noise while processing the elongated input. In contrast, VerDICt requests for answerability feedback per each passage in UqU_{q}, using only a single passage as a context.

subsections.title: 4.2 Consolidated Feedbacks
paragraphs: While retriever and generator feedback are essential for verification, both are prone to noise. For robust verification, we propose to consolidate retriever and generator feedback into a unified, robust signal, without additional retrieval or inference calls.

Specifically, question-answer pair from generator are projected into a latent space ℝd\mathbb{R}^{d} using an encoder ff from the retriever (Figure 2). We then cluster similar pairs, to choose those consistently supported by relevant passages, while filtering out outliers from noisy passages. In this process, multiple generator and retriever feedback signals are consolidated, which aligns with seeking the most consistent outputs (Chen et al., 2023), improving robustness by inference scaling of aggregating multiple LLM outputs. Additionally, selecting a medoid question per cluster deduplicates redundant interpretations, whereas DtV defers deduplication to the answer generation stage in Eq. 4.
While DtV uses similarity for pruning UU, we use both retriever and generator feedback, for another purpose of consolidation, to leverage richer signals including interaction between the two.

subsections.title: 4.3 Grounding in Evaluation
paragraphs: This section discusses existing evaluation metrics, contrasted with grounded evaluation, often required in enterprise RAG scenarios.

Current benchmarks (Min et al., 2020; Stelmakh et al., 2022) measure whether diverse human-annotated interpretations “match” model-generated ones, typically using recall as the primary metric. Lexical similarity (e.g., BLEU) determines the match between generated (q^\hat{q}) and reference (q~\tilde{q}) interpretations:
We classify this as ungrounded evaluation, as it does not assess whether retrieved passage supports the generated interpretation.

In enterprise RAG, answer verifiability—measured through whether answer can be supported by the correct citation of a supporting passage in the corpus (Li et al., 2023a; Liu et al., 2023)—has been a evaluation criterion. This concept naturally extends to verify disambiguation, where an interpretation is validated based on whether its answer can be supported by a passage in the corpus, as a binary evaluation function below:
where p^\hat{p} is predicted as the supporting passage associated with q^\hat{q} by the model.
With this extended VV, grounded precision is defined as the ratio of correctly grounded among model-generated interpretations:
While optimizing for existing recall may encourage ungrounded diversity, pursuing G-precision counterbalances this by assessing whether the interpretation is accurately grounded.

Similarly, we extend to define a grounded recall metric that evaluates model-generated interpretations, only against the grounded gold set, 𝒬¯\bar{\mathcal{Q}}. This ensures that generating ungrounded interpretations, such as “Harry Potter,” is penalized in G-Recall. As a result, models that prioritize ungrounded diversity will experience a significant drop when transitioning from ungrounded recall to G-Recall.
We provide further details on how we obtain 𝒬¯\bar{\mathcal{Q}} in Appendix A.
We empirically validate VerDICt ensures human-level diversity, while balancing with grounding accuracy. We ablate different components to show their contributions and also analyze errors.

subsections.title: 5.1 Experimental Settings
paragraphs: To evaluate diversity, we report the average number of generated interpretations per query |𝒬^||\hat{\mathcal{Q}}|, the proportion of queries with sufficient diversity, denoted as Sufficient%444We deduplicate the generated interpretations using the prompt IDI_{\textrm{D}} in Figure 9 and count how many unique interpretations are found. and (ungrounded) recall, all computed against human interpretations without grounding.

For grounded metrics, we use our agentic evaluation protocol described in Section 4.3 to compute G-precision, G-recall, and G-F1\textrm{F}_{1} score defined as the harmonic mean of the precision and recall, balancing the objective of two.

We consider the ASQA benchmark (Stelmakh et al., 2022) as our main target dataset for evaluation. It is built upon the ambiguous question qq from the AmbigNQ benchmark (Min et al., 2020), adding: (1) 2-6 human-annotated interpretations 𝒬~\tilde{\mathcal{Q}}, along with corresponding answers 𝒴~\tilde{\mathcal{Y}}, (2) optionally attached with a supporting passage p~i\tilde{p}_{i} per each interpretation q~i\tilde{q}_{i} from the corpus CC, Wikipedia. We utilize its validation split, which consists of 948 ambiguous questions with list of human-annotated interpretations attached to each question, list of answers to those questions, and also long-form answers composed by aggregating those answers.

The retrieval system comprises arctic-embed (Merrick et al., 2024)555Snowflake/snowflake-arctic-embed-m-v2.0 based first-phase retrieval and second-phase passage reranking using gte-Qwen2 (Li et al., 2023b).666Alibaba-NLP/gte-Qwen2-7B-instruct

For the clustering algorithm for consolidation phase described in Section 4.2, we used HDBSCAN (Campello et al., 2013), a hierarchical density-based clustering algorithm. As the encoder ff for obtaining embeddings, we reused the same gte-Qwen2 embedding model used in retrieval. For decoding, greedy decoding was used to obtain deterministic responses from LLMs.

For verification in DtV, we fixed the verifier as the most expensive model, GPT-4o, as less capable 8B and 70B models struggled to provide reliable results for long-context inputs, damaging its performance severely. More details can be found in Appendix D.

While we mainly compare VerDICt against DtV and its ablated version as baseline, we also consider retrieval-augmented clarification (RAC; Kim et al., 2023) as contrasted in Table 3 which leverages relevance feedback in diversification similar to VerDICt:

Our distinction is first extracting (q^,y^)(\hat{q},\hat{y}) then jointly verifying relevance and answerability, while RAC evaluates the entire UqU_{q} with a single long-context LLM inference—negative impact of such decision is empirically discussed in Section 5.2.

subsections.title: 5.2 Results
paragraphs: In this section, we validate the effectiveness and efficiency of VerDICt in disambiguating ambiguous questions, answering the following research questions:
• RQ1: Is VerDICt diverse?
• RQ2: Does diversity balance with grounding?
• RQ3: What is the effect of consolidation phase, and how is it tunable?
RQ1: Is VerDICt diverse?
RQ2: Does diversity balance with grounding?
RQ3: What is the effect of consolidation phase, and how is it tunable?

Table 2 reports scores of VerDICt, human-annotation, RAC (Kim et al., 2023), and DtV (In et al., 2024), in terms of diversity metrics ‘|𝒬^||\hat{\mathcal{Q}}|’, ‘Sufficient%’, and ‘Recall’ (Section 5.1.1).

Note, in all metrics, VerDICt’s output is comparable to human interpretation, and remains consistent across diverse models sizes. In contrast, diversity of interpretations from DtV and RAC tend to be highly affected by model choice/size. For example, with smaller model, 8B, the output becomes noticeably less diverse, e.g., Sufficient% is dropped to <1%. This validates VerDICt generalizes better for smaller models and performs consistently over model sizes, by decomposing context size to a single passage and verifying each y^\hat{y} at a time, and allowing separate reasoning on each passage.
While ungrounded recall scores are generally higher for DtV, we show the negative consequences of incentivizing ungrounded diversity in the next section.
Table 2 shows ungrounded metrics fail to ground to corpus: 35% of human-annoated fall into such category, which will incentivize recall when matches are found from generated interpretations.

It also contrasts ungrounded metrics, with verifiable metrics G-precision, G-recall and G-F1\textrm{F}_{1}, to show distinctions. VerDICt achieves the highest G-precision, of 93% with GPT-4o and 81% with LLaMA 70B as the backbone LLM.

These results reinforce our earlier discussion in Section 4.3: DtV without verification, despite achieving higher ungrounded recall, suffers from the lowest G-precision across all backbone LLMs, highlighting its tendency to generate unverified interpretations. In contrast, both RAC and VerDICt produce more accurately grounded interpretations, with VerDICt further benefiting from both retriever and generator feedback. The G-recall results confirm this advantage, showing that interpretations q^i\hat{q}_{i} generated by VerDICt are well-supported by their corresponding passages p^i\hat{p}_{i}, ultimately boosting grounded recall to approximately 57%.

Finally, to balance the conflicting optimization objective of precision and recall, G-F1\textrm{F}_{1} is reported. G-F1\textrm{F}_{1} scores show clear gap in performance between VerDICt and baselines for the goal of pursuing both accurately grounded and diverse interpretations. For instance, while G-precision of RAC is higher with the 8B backbone, it fails to ensure enough diversity, where the average number of interpretations obtained per question is less than 1. This leads to significantly lower G-recall and thus G-F1\textrm{F}_{1} score, reflecting its failure to provide meaningful disambiguations of the original ambiguous question.

To continue with our RQ3, Table 4 (top half) shows the consolidation step in VerDICt can be parameterized to control the number of resulting interpretations. Specifically, it can be adjusted to favor less clusters, denoted as ‘conservative.’ DtV approaches do not provide such knobs to control, as deciding the number of resulting interpretations is up to the LLM.

Table 4 (bottom half) also shows VerDICt can control how to embed a clustering space. By default, VerDICt concatenates interpretation q^\hat{q} and answer y^\hat{y}, then encodes it with an encoder ff to obtain f​(q^;y^)f(\hat{q};\hat{y}), utilizing match between answers or the accordance of question-answer. It is shown that embedding questions only also achieves comparable performance, suggesting interpretations from VerDICt are already reliable, as VerDICt already pruned much of the noisy questions and passages during diversification.

We conclude our discussion with error analysis: Figure 3 shows categorization (q^,y^)(\hat{q},\hat{y}) pairs generated from VerDICt. Specifically, an LLM judge decided whether q^\hat{q} is relevant to the original question qq, and whether q^\hat{q} can be answered with p^\hat{p}.

First, the top-left corner of each plot in Figure 3 illustrates cases where the retrieved passage is both relevant and answerable. The 8B model correctly answers 92% of these instances, while stronger models achieve even higher accuracy, reaching 98% and 99%, respectively.

Second, the remaining L-shaped sections depict scenarios where the passage p^\hat{p} is either irrelevant or unanswerable. These numbers indicate the proportion of incorrect answers due to ungrounded interpretations, showing smaller models struggling more. However, these errors are well mitigated with scaling model size, where the 70B model cut errors by more than half and GPT-4o reduces them to less than one-sixth, compared to 8B.

Figure 4 illustrates a real-world deployment of our method within Snowflake’s Cortex Agents API, highlighting its practical impact. In this production setting, our approach is applied to a synthetic corpus for an insurance company analyzing repair invoices related to auto insurance claims.

The figure demonstrates how a vague inquiry, such as “rental cars,” is disambiguated into grounded clarifications. Each clarified question is grounded to the cited passages that can answer the question, obtained from the verification process (Eq. 5). This approach significantly enhances retrieval accuracy and improves the overall user experience.

We question the common workflow of DtV for grounding ambiguous questions in RAG. We proposed VerDICt, which more efficiently and effectively grounds diversification to the retrieval corpus, ensuring verifiability of the generated interpretations and answers. Both ungrounded and grounded evaluation empirically validated our method significantly improves groundedness, while balancing with human-level diversity.
Though we propose consolidation to handle noisy feedback, we have not considered extreme cases where the retriever or generator performs unreasonably poor or adversarially.

This would impact the initial retrieval step, where an adaptive scheme can mitigate by better balancing the retrieval breadth (with additional calls) and efficiency. Developing an approach that allows the agent to dynamically adapt, based on feedback quality, remains an open challenge for future work.

Here, we explain in more detail how our evaluation protocol identified matching pairs for computing precision and recall. We begin by reviewing the setting of previous works and how original precision/recall has been computed in ambiguous question answering evaluation.

[Figure: Figure 5: Comparison of end-to-end workflow of VerDICt and DtV (DIVA; In et al., 2024) for handling ambiguous question answering task. Vertical arrangement denotes sequential dependency, while calls that can run in parallel are placed at the same horizontal level. ]

Figure 5 describes how an ambiguous question is answered, with our VerDICt and DIVA (In et al., 2024), a representative of Diversify-then-Verify (DtV) workflow. DtV involves more sequential steps, verifying the relevance of passages post hoc while in VerDICt verification is integrated into diversification, as shown on the left.

In this section, we provide the instruction prompts provided to LLMs. For reproducing baselines, we reused their prompts, IPI_{\textrm{P}} for generating pseudo-interpretations (In et al., 2024) or IGI_{\textrm{G}} for generating a list of interpretations and answers at once (Kim et al., 2023), which can be found in their respective papers.
Figure 6 shows the prompt used to generate interpretation and answer in VerDICt, as described in Eq. 8. Figure 7 and 8 present prompts used for evaluation, as discussed in detail in Appendix A.
[Figure: Figure 6: Prompt IEI_{\textrm{E}} for obtaining interpretation q^\hat{q} and answer y^\hat{y} with execution feedback from the LLM.]
[Figure: Figure 7: Prompt IMI_{\textrm{M}} for determining matches between q^\hat{q}’s and q~\tilde{q}’s.]
[Figure: Figure 8: Prompt IVI_{\textrm{V}} for determining a match between q^\hat{q} and p^\hat{p}.]
[Figure: Figure 9: Prompt IDI_{\textrm{D}} for removing (near-)duplicates in a list of interpretations for an ambiguous question.]

Here, we provide more details regarding the experimental settings. For main experiments, VerDICt (ours) and RAC have used top-20 passages, retrieved with relaxed question q′q^{\prime} as the search query. For DtV, we have used the same encoder, Qwen, for pruning the passages and then fixed verifier LLM as GPT-4o, as mentioned previously in Section 5.1.3. Following the original paper’s setting, we retrieved top-5 passages for each pseudo-interpretation, then finally selected top-5 among them, after pruning.
